scope: Round-1 manual encoding: only variant pairs whose difference is PURE relational algebra are proved with sql_equiv. Data/hypothesis-dependent, NULL, rounding-precision, and top-N ordering differences are classified out_of_scope (a future hypothesis phase).
encoding_form: Parser-canonical: schema as `List SQLTypeProxy`, types via colTypeOfList/TypedRelationOfList, DecidableEq via sqlTypeDecEq. Full table column sets are encoded (not just columns used).

outcome_legend.pass: sql_equiv closed the winnable pure-algebra difference (file has an active proof).
outcome_legend.out_of_scope: no pure-algebra equivalence between variants; needs a data hypothesis, or differs only by NULL/rounding/top-N ordering. Documented in-file, no theorem.
outcome_legend.fail: a genuinely-algebraic goal sql_equiv could not close (none remain this round; the join-commutativity gap was filled — see lemmas_added_to_toolbox).
outcome_legend.pass_under_hypothesis: true equivalence only under a stated data hypothesis (e.g. a functional dependency); proved with that hypothesis as an explicit premise via the FD/constraint layer.

lemmas_added_to_toolbox.name: crossProduct_comm + swapAppend (+ splitTuple_swapAppend, swapAppend_swapAppend involution)
lemmas_added_to_toolbox.reason: join-commutativity gap surfaced while encoding the join-order family
lemmas_added_to_toolbox.statement: (crossProductRel r1 r2 a1 a2).rows.image swapAppend = (crossProductRel r2 r1 a2 a1).rows; swapAppend is the dependent half-swap reindex (c1++c2)->(c2++c1) and an involution.

lemmas_added_to_toolbox.name: join_comm
lemmas_added_to_toolbox.statement: (join r1 r2 a1 a2 cond).rows.image swapAppend = (join r2 r1 a2 a1 (fun u => cond (swapAppend u))).rows.

lemmas_added_to_toolbox.name: FD + cnt_eq_of_partition_eq + cnt_pair_eq_of_FD
lemmas_added_to_toolbox.reason: turn data-dependent GROUP-BY / projection equivalences into provable-under-hypothesis theorems
lemmas_added_to_toolbox.statement: FD key det R := all rows agreeing on key agree on det. cnt_pair_eq_of_FD: GROUP BY key ≡ GROUP BY (det,key) counts when key→det.

lemmas_added_to_toolbox.name: select_congr
lemmas_added_to_toolbox.statement: two computed SELECTs are equal if their row-maps agree on every input row (bridge for FD-based projection rewrites).

instances.difference: the VARIABLE_GROUP filter is written as a SQL IN (…) list (baseline-sg) vs. an explicit OR-chain of equalities (probing). Everything else (join, GROUP BY DATE, SUM, ORDER BY) is identical, so the whole-query equivalence follows by congruence from the equality of the two WHERE predicates — proved her

probing vs baseline-sg. Everything else (the LEFT JOIN LATERAL FLATTEN, WHERE grant_date BETWEEN 20100101 AND 20181231, GROUP BY

semantic-probing writes the recovery rate as SUM(recovered) / SUM(confirmed) 100 (divide then multiply); probing writes SUM(recovered) 100.0 / SUM(confirmed) (multiply then divide). All four wrap it in ROUND(_,2); GROUP BY country, HAVING SUM(confirmed)>50000, ORDER BY … LIMIT 3 are shared.

(data functional dependency). All four variants use the same join order (BIRTH_DEATH_GROWTH_RATES b JOIN COUNTRY_NAMES_AREA); the only real difference is whether the output country_name is read from b (BIRTH_DEATH…) or from c/a (COUNTRY_NAMES_AREA). Those agree only when country_code → country_name
instances.note: Now PROVED under the cross-table FD: ON forces matching country_code on each joined row, FD then forces matching country_name, so SELECT b.country_name ≡ SELECT c.country_name (select_congr).

(needs a data hypothesis). All three variants share the same JOIN, WHERE reputation > 10, and ORDER BY COUNT() DESC LIMIT 1. The substantive difference is the GROUP BY granularity: - semantic-probing: GROUP BY display_name - probing / baseline-1: GROUP BY id, display_name
instances.note: Now PROVED under the FD hypothesis via cnt_pair_eq_of_FD (Constraints.lean): GROUP BY display_name and GROUP BY id,display_name give equal per-group COUNT(*).

instances.difference: both variants compute the per-category education-share as SUM(CASE WHEN LOWER(body) LIKE '%education%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) over COUNT(); they differ only in the ratio order — x 100 / total (semantic-probing) vs 100 x / total (baseline-sg). semantic-probing vs probing. Identical WHERE (tags LIKE '%python-2%' AND tags NOT LIKE '%python-3%') and identical MAX(...); the only difference is the aggregated expression: MAX(CAST("answer_count" AS INTEGER)) vs MAX("answer_count").

NONE — OUT-OF-SCOPE (data-dependent LIKE patterns). Across the four variants there are three independent differences: - the late-order ratio order: SUM(CASE…) 100 / COUNT() vs 100 SUM(CASE…) / COUNT() — pure algebra ( commutes), the pair proved here; - semantic-probing adds shippeddate IS NOT NULL to the predi
